questions.scenario: A GenAI application uses Guardrails, but the team notices some edge cases aren't handled by the standard filters. They need to implement company-specific validation rules that Guardrails can't express.
questions.question: How should custom validation rules complement Guardrails?

options.text: Use Lambda post-processing for custom rules while keeping Guardrails for standard filtering
Replace Guardrails with entirely custom Lambda-based filtering
Request AWS to add custom filters to Guardrails
Add all custom rules to the system prompt

questions.explanation: Keep Guardrails for managed content filtering (harmful content, PII, denied topics) and add Lambda post-processing for custom business rules. This combines managed capabilities with flexibility for company-specific requirements.

incorrectExplanations.a: AWS doesn't add custom filters for individual customers. Guardrails provides configurable but general capabilities.
incorrectExplanations.b: Replacing Guardrails loses managed filtering capabilities. Use both for defense-in-depth.
incorrectExplanations.d: System prompt rules are unreliable for validation. They can be bypassed or ignored.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Custom + standard = Lambda post-processing + Guardrails

questions.examTip: Combine Guardrails (managed filtering) with Lambda (custom rules) for comprehensive coverage.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application logs all model invocations to CloudWatch Logs. The team needs to quickly find all invocations where guardrails blocked content in the past 24 hours.
questions.question: Which service enables querying log data for specific patterns?

options.text: CloudTrail event history
CloudWatch Logs Insights with query language
AWS X-Ray tracing
Amazon Athena on log files

questions.explanation: CloudWatch Logs Insights provides a query language for searching and analyzing log data. You can filter for specific patterns like guardrail blocks, aggregate results, and visualize trends directly from CloudWatch.

incorrectExplanations.d: CloudTrail logs API calls, not application-level events like guardrail triggers.
incorrectExplanations.c: X-Ray traces application requests, not log content. It doesn't query log patterns.
incorrectExplanations.b: Athena requires logs to be in S3 and setting up tables. It's more complex for simple log queries.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Log queries = CloudWatch Logs Insights

questions.examTip: Logs Insights queries log data directly. No need to export to S3 for simple analysis.

questions.scenario: A company stores model invocation logs that may contain customer PII in the prompts and responses. They need to ensure logs are retained for compliance but PII exposure is minimized.
questions.question: How should invocation logging be configured for PII-safe compliance?

options.text: Log to public CloudWatch Log groups for easy access
Disable logging entirely to avoid PII exposure
Log full invocations encrypted with customer-managed KMS keys to a locked-down S3 bucket
Log only metadata (model ID, timestamp, token counts) without prompts/responses

questions.explanation: Encrypted logging with customer-managed keys provides compliance (full audit trail) while protecting PII (encryption, access control). Store in S3 with restricted access. This balances compliance requirements with privacy protection.

incorrectExplanations.d: Public log groups expose PII. Access must be strictly controlled.
incorrectExplanations.a: Disabling logging fails compliance requirements. Auditors need invocation records.
incorrectExplanations.c: Metadata-only logging may not satisfy compliance requirements for full audit trails.

eliminationHints: Metadata only = may not satisfy auditors
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Compliant + PII-safe = encrypted logging with access control

questions.examTip: Balance compliance (logging) with privacy (encryption, access control).

questions.scenario: A team suspects their customer support AI responds differently based on customer name patterns that might correlate with ethnicity. They want to systematically test for this bias.
questions.question: Which approach tests for bias at scale?

options.text: Manually review a sample of responses
Ask the model if it has any biases
Use Bedrock Model Evaluations with LLM-as-a-Judge to evaluate responses across diverse test cases
Check the model provider's documentation for known biases

questions.explanation: LLM-as-a-Judge can evaluate responses for bias indicators at scale. Create test datasets with diverse name patterns, run evaluations, and have the judge model assess for differential treatment. This provides systematic, scalable bias testing.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual review doesn't scale and may miss subtle patterns across large datasets.
incorrectExplanations.d: Provider documentation covers general biases, not your specific application's behavior.
incorrectExplanations.c: Models aren't reliable sources about their own biases. Self-assessment doesn't work.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Systematic bias testing = LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations

questions.examTip: LLM-as-a-Judge enables scalable bias evaluation across diverse test cases.

questions.scenario: Before deploying a GenAI application to production, the security team wants to test its defenses against known attack patterns including prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data extraction attempts.
questions.question: What is this type of proactive security testing called?

options.text: Unit testing
Adversarial testing or red teaming
User acceptance testing
Load testing

questions.explanation: Adversarial testing (red teaming) proactively probes security defenses using known attack patterns. Test datasets include prompt injections, jailbreak attempts, and data extraction attacks. This finds vulnerabilities before attackers do.

incorrectExplanations.d: User acceptance testing verifies features meet requirements, not security posture.
incorrectExplanations.a: Unit testing verifies code functionality, not security against attacks.
incorrectExplanations.c: Load testing measures performance under high traffic, not security.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Security attack testing = adversarial testing / red teaming

questions.examTip: Adversarial testing proactively finds security vulnerabilities before deployment.

questions.scenario: A model has been updated with new training data. The compliance team needs to track which version of the model is deployed in production and access documentation for that specific version.
questions.question: Which service links model versions to their documentation?

options.text: Git tags for model code
DynamoDB for version tracking
SageMaker Model Registry with Model Cards attached to versions
S3 versioning for model artifacts

questions.explanation: SageMaker Model Registry tracks model versions with approval status. Model Cards attach to specific versions, providing documentation (purpose, limitations, ethics) linked to each version. This creates auditable, version-linked documentation.

incorrectExplanations.c: Git tags track code versions but don't provide model documentation framework.
incorrectExplanations.d: DynamoDB would require building custom version tracking. Model Registry is purpose-built.
incorrectExplanations.b: S3 versioning tracks file versions but doesn't link to structured documentation.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Version-linked documentation = Model Registry + Model Cards

questions.examTip: Model Registry versions link to Model Cards for auditable documentation.

questions.scenario: A company wants to enable model invocation logging for their Bedrock application. They need logs stored securely with their own encryption keys for compliance.
questions.question: Where should Bedrock model invocation logs be stored for maximum security control?

options.text: S3 bucket encrypted with customer-managed KMS keys
Default CloudWatch Logs
Local application logs
Third-party logging service

questions.explanation: S3 with customer-managed KMS keys gives you full control over encryption and access. You manage the key policy, can revoke access, and maintain complete audit trails. This meets strict compliance requirements.

incorrectExplanations.a: Default CloudWatch uses AWS-managed encryption. You don't control the keys.
incorrectExplanations.d: Third-party services may not meet compliance requirements for data residency and control.
incorrectExplanations.c: Local logs lack durability, security, and centralized access control.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Maximum control = S3 with customer-managed KMS

questions.examTip: Customer-managed KMS keys provide maximum control over encryption.

questions.scenario: A company's AI ethics board has defined policies including 'all AI decisions affecting customers over $1000 require human approval' and 'no medical advice.' Engineering needs to implement these consistently.
questions.question: How should ethics board policies be translated into technical controls?

options.text: Document policies in a wiki for reference
Implement Guardrails for content policies (medical advice) and approval workflows for high-value decisions
Train all developers on the policies
Add all policies to the system prompt

questions.explanation: Guardrails enforce content policies (denied topics like medical advice) at runtime. Approval workflows enforce process policies (human review for high-value decisions). Together they translate ethics policies into enforceable technical controls.

incorrectExplanations.b: Documentation informs but doesn't enforce. Policies can still be violated.
incorrectExplanations.a: Training relies on human compliance and doesn't enforce policies technically.
incorrectExplanations.d: System prompts are unreliable for policy enforcement. They can be bypassed or ignored.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Policy enforcement = Guardrails (content) + workflows (process)

questions.examTip: Translate ethics policies into technical controls: Guardrails for content, workflows for process.

questions.scenario: A RAG system generates answers with citations. The team wants to verify that citations actually support the claims made in the response, detecting when the model cites a document but makes claims not found in it.
questions.question: How should citation accuracy be validated?

options.text: Count the number of citations as a quality metric
Trust that the model cites correctly since it retrieved the documents
Ask users to verify citations manually
Use contextual grounding checks to verify claims are supported by cited context

questions.explanation: Contextual grounding checks compare response claims against the retrieved context. If claims aren't supported by the cited documents, grounding scores are low. This catches hallucinated claims even when citations exist.

incorrectExplanations.d: Manual verification doesn't scale and burdens users with validation work.
incorrectExplanations.c: Citation count doesn't indicate accuracy. Many citations can still be misattributed.
incorrectExplanations.a: Models can cite documents but make claims not in them. Retrieval doesn't guarantee accurate citation.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Citation validation = contextual grounding checks

questions.examTip: Contextual grounding validates that citations actually support the claims made.
